steps.title: Use the context
steps.content: Nature threw every conceivable obstacle in the way — meaning every kind of obstacle one could think of. The phrase emphasizes the wide range of difficulties.

steps.title: Match the meaning
steps.content:
"Conceivable" here needs to mean able to be thought of or imagined — nature threw every kind of obstacle one could imagine. Substitute each choice:
  • Choice A — Persuasive: "every persuasive obstacle" — persuasive means convincing. Obstacles don't convince; they block. ✗
  • Choice B — Permanent: "every permanent obstacle" — permanent means lasting forever. The passage emphasizes the range of obstacles, not how long-lasting they were. ✗
  • Choice C — Obvious: "every obvious obstacle" — obvious means easy to see. The passage stresses completeness of variety, not visibility. ✗
  • Choice D — Imaginable: "every imaginable obstacle" — imaginable means able to be thought of. This captures "conceivable" exactly: every kind of obstacle one could conceive of. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
